content: use for what?
it is just normal rust binary, not involved kernel things. Is it?
Hostprogs are software that run on the build machine, as opposed to being part of the kernel
so, what is the difference between the rustforlinux hostprogs intruduced and any rust hostprog we code before?
i guess that rustforlinux hostprogs use the rust library tailored for kernel? and any rust hostprog we code before just use normal rust library, is that right?
What is the context? At least one difference is no cargo
rustforlinux hostprogs mean hostsprogs rustforlinux project intruduced; any rust hostprog mean any userspace rust binary. Since no diference, iam curious why rustforlinux intruduce this concept?
Are you asking the difference between a "rustforlinux hostprog" and a "rust hostprog"? Where do you see this difference?
i have no idea the rustforlinux hostprog, so ask here.
I have never heard of, specifically, "rustforlinux hostprog". "hostprog" means a program that lives in the kernel repo but runs on the build machine, and since RFL these can be written in Rust
What are you reading or seeing that you are trying to understand?
I wouldn't call any Rust binary a "hostprog" if it has nothing to do with kernel, if that's what you mean
see the commit 9a8ff24ce584ad9895f9416fe8fad6f8842f758d

It also serves as an example of a Rust host program

Are you referring to this quote?
He just means a host program written in Rust. So something that lives in the linux repo but runs on the build machine, so it isn't a part of vmlinux
see also samples/rust/hostprogs/Makefile
What is your question?
That is a sample hostprog, written in Rust
i am missed that normal rust binary is just so popular, why intruduce again this concep in the kernel? and add a example for hostprog?
it is no diffrent with the rust binary we read or code when learn rust language.
So ask why intruduce? maybe have some difference?

By "introduce" do you mean why are there hostprogs in the kernel at all?

It doesn't matter if they're written in C or Rust. Hostprogs are compiled programs that do something related to the kernel but just aren't part of the kernel image. Things like:

  • Build configuration
  • Generate source files
  • Test setup / running

They are compiled by kbuild. Before RFL, you would write hostprogs in C but now, you can also write them in Rust
The code is no different than any other Rust binary that you might write. The source is just in linux and they are built by the kernel build system
Maybe this section helps? https://docs.kernel.org/kbuild/makefiles.html#host-program-support
Oh, thank your help. i understand . The link clean my confused.
No problem!
